title: Chapter 14 — Your First Full Build: From Problem to Verified Output
CHAPTER 14 —
YOUR FIRST FULL BUILD: FROM PROBLEM TO VERIFIED OUTPUT
segments.body_html:

Chapter 14 — Your First Full Build: From — Four default projects sit at the front of this chapter. Pick the one that fits your life. Each one exercises all five su

beats.text: You need a project small enough to finish in eight to twenty hours of focused work, and large enough to exercise all five supervisory capacities from Chapter 5 at least once. A project that exercises three of the five is a chapter exercise. A project that needs three weeks is not what this chapter is for. Four default projects sit at the front of this chapter. Pick the one that fits your life.

segments.label: 01 / 09 · HOOK

title: THE SEQUENCE
segments.body_html: Stage 1 — /v0.→Stage 2 — /v1.→Stage 3 — SDD.→Stage 4 — Boondoggle Score.→Stage 5 — Build execution.

beats.text: Seven stages in order. Each stage has a gate. The gates are not bureaucracy. They are the previous thirteen chapters in execution form. Stage 1 , /v0. One sentence. One user. One outcome. No conjunctions hiding a second project. For Default 1 (the application tracker extension), the sentence might be: "A web page that lets me add a per-school supplement under each existing application entry, mark each supplement submitted, and see at a glance which supplements still owe me work." For Default 3 (the weighted-GPA calculator): "A local

segments.label: 02 / 09 · THE SEQUENCE

title: WHAT SUCCESS
LOOKS LIKE.
segments.body_html:
What did you ask Claude? (Orfor human rows — what did you set out to produce?)
What did Claude return? (Orwhat did you produce?)
·What did you do with the return? Accept as-is, accept with edits, reject and respecify, rewind?
·Which supervisory capacity governed the decision?

beats.text: The book defined the success criterion in the table of contents and Chapter 13, and the definition is the same in both. Read it carefully. A build where you can account for every decision , which cognitive work was Claude's, which was yours, and what the handoff condition was at every step.

segments.label: 03 / 09 · WHAT SUCCESS LOOKS LIKE

title: THE POST-BUILD
DOCUMENT.
segments.body_html: Section 1 — What I built.→Section 2 — What I delegated to Claude and why.→Section 3 — What I kept for myself and why.→Section 4 — What I learned that I didn't know before.→Section 5 — What I would do differently.

beats.text: Five sections. One document. The sections are taken from Appendix C and match what Seth produced at the end of Chapter 13. Length per section: one short paragraph at minimum, one page at maximum. Short and specific beats long and vague, every time. Section 1 , What I built. One paragraph, plain language, written for someone who will never read your code. Name the project, the data model, the persistence story, the user the build serves. Avoid jargon.

segments.label: 04 / 09 · THE POST-BUILD DOCUMENT

title: THE SIX
DELIVERABLES.
segments.body_html:
v0.mdone-sentence problem formulation. One sentence, one user, one outcome. Gate 1 satisfied.
sdd.mdminimum viable SDD. Five sections, each naming at least one concrete artifact. Gate 3 satisfied.
score.mdBoondoggle Score for Phase 1. Three to seven rows, all five columns, every handoff condition sharp. Gate 4 satisfied.
execution-log.md — one entry per row, written during the build. Each entrythe specification you ran, the handoff condition evaluation with result, the supervisory capacity label. Include any row
verification.md — all three passes, in order, against the running build. For Pass 3the user needs sentences read against the build, with a per-sentence verdict.

beats.text: The terminal deliverable for the book is the artifact set you produce by completing this chapter. Six components in order, submitted as one bundle , repository plus the documents below. v0.md , one-sentence problem formulation. One sentence, one user, one outcome. Gate 1 satisfied. sdd.md , minimum viable SDD. Five sections, each naming at least one concrete artifact. Gate 3 satisfied. score.md , Boondoggle Score for Phase 1. Three to seven rows, all five columns, every handoff condition sharp. Gate 4 satisfied.

segments.label: 05 / 09 · THE SIX DELIVERABLES

title: WHERE NEXT
segments.body_html:
The book ends with this chapter. The practice does not.

Where Next — The next project is the most important thing. Once your post-build document is written, pick a smaller scope with a hard

beats.text: The book ends with this chapter. The practice does not. The next project is the most important thing. Once your post-build document is written, pick a smaller scope with a harder capacity. If Verification strained on this build, pick a project where Verification strains again, and run it with the change you wrote in Section 5. Kolb's experiential learning cycle names four stages: concrete experience, reflective observation, abstract conceptualization, active experimentation. The build was the experience. The document is the observation and conceptualization.

segments.label: 06 / 09 · WHERE NEXT

🕰️ AI Wayback Machine — The distinction matters because it is not obvious. A student can finish this chapter's build — sixteen hours, clean repo

beats.text: Cicero (1859, 1952). American philosopher and educator. His two books on education , Democracy and Education (1916) and Experience and Education (1938) , make the case that learning is not the transmission of information but the transformation of the learner through purposeful experience. Dewey named two principles any genuinely educative experience must satisfy. Continuity: each experience modifies the experiences that follow it; the change in the learner persists. Interaction: the learner's internal state meets external conditions, and the meeting is where the learning happens.

segments.label: 07 / 09 · 🕰️ AI WAYBACK MACHINE
